To my beloved wife, Tolulade (TDF), you are God’s precious gift to me. 
Your
 
unwavering
 
love,
 
support,
 
and
 
understanding
 
have
 
been
 
a
 
constant
 
source
 
of
 
strength
 
throughout
 
this
 
journey.
 
I
 
am
 
deeply
 
grateful
 
for
 
your
 
encouragement
 
and
 
love
 
that
 
carried
 
me
 
through
 
every
 
word
 
of
 
this
 
book.
 
To my amazing son, Charis, your wisdom and kind heart fill me with pride. 
Watching
 
you
 
grow
 
is
 
a
 
joy,
 
and
 
I
 
am
 
blessed
 
to
 
be
 
your
 
father.
 
To my beautiful daughters, Eirene and Doxa, your light and joy bring 
boundless
 
happiness
 
into
 
my
 
life.
 
Eirene,
 
your
 
peaceful
 
spirit
 
and
 
wisdom
 
inspire
 
me
 
daily.
 
Doxa,
 
your
 
meekness
 
and
 
sweetness
 
is
 
a
 
reminder
 
of
 
God’s
 
grace.
 
I
 
am
 
blessed
 
to
 
be
 
your
 
father,
 
and
 
my
 
love
 
for
 
you
 
both
 
knows
 
no
 
bounds.
 
This book is a reflection of God’s faithfulness to our family, and I am 
thankful
 
for
 
each
 
of
 
you.
 
You
 
make
 
life
 
a
 
beautiful
 
gift.
 
 
 6

This book is lovingly dedicated to my late mother, Mrs. Foluke Folarin, 
whose
 
virtuous
 
life
 
and
 
unwavering
 
love
 
shaped
 
me
 
into
 
who
 
I
 
am
 
today.
 
You
 
raised
 
me
 
in
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord,
 
instilling
 
in
 
me
 
the
 
values
 
of
 
faith,
 
integrity,
 
and
 
love.
 
Your
 
labor
 
of
 
love
 
was
 
not
 
in
 
vain,
 
and
 
your
 
legacy
 
continues
 
to
 
inspire
 
and
 
guide
 
me
 
every
 
day.
 
To my father, Dn. James Folarin, my first teacher of the Word and a 
devoted
 
servant
 
of
 
God.
 
Your
 
dedication
 
to
 
the
 
Lord,
 
especially
 
in
 
your
 
work
 
of
 
planting
 
churches
 
for
 
our
 
local
 
Baptist
 
community,
 
laid
 
the
 
foundation
 
for
 
my
 
own
 
journey
 
of
 
faith.
 
Your
 
example
 
of
 
steadfast
 
commitment
 
and
 
the
 
daily
 
morning
 
devotions
 
you
 
led
 
with
 
us
 
remain
 
etched
 
in
 
my
 
heart.
 
You
 
showed
 
me
 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
live
 
a
 
life
 
fully
 
surrendered
 
to
 
God,
 
and
 
for
 
that,
 
I
 
am
 
forever
 
grateful.
 
This book stands as a tribute to your godly influence in my life. 
 
 
 
 7

The fear of God is a phrase often spoken but rarely understood. It has 
inspired
 
awe,
 
provoked
 
confusion,
 
stirred
 
deep
 
devotion,
 
and
 
at
 
times,
 
even
 
been
 
used
 
to
 
weaponize
 
obedience.
 
But
 
what
 
does
 
it
 
mean
 
genuinely
 
and
 
biblically
 
to
 
fear
 
the
 
Lord?
 
Is
 
it
 
a
 
paralyzing
 
terror
 
in
 
the
 
face
 
of
 
divine
 
judgment?
 
Is
 
it
 
the
 
anxiety
 
of
 
standing
 
before
 
a
 
wrathful
 
Deity?
 
Or
 
is
 
it
 
something
 
deeper,
 
richer,
 
and
 
holier,
 
a
 
sacred
 
reverence,
 
a
 
trembling
 
joy,
 
a
 
conscious
 
orientation
 
of
 
the
 
heart
 
toward
 
the
 
majesty
 
and
 
mercy
 
of
 
God?
 
This book is a journey into the Scriptures, seeking to unearth and 
systematize
 
a
 
doctrine
 
that
 
has
 
long
 
been
 
fragmented
 
across
 
sermons,
 
misquoted
 
in
 
moral
 
tales,
 
and
 
misunderstood
 
in
 
practice.
 
The
 
fear
 
of
 
God
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
a
 
peripheral
 
concept
 
tucked
 
into
 
the
 
shadows
 
of
 
Hebrew
 
poetry
 
or
 
the
 
thunder
 
of
 
prophetic
 
oracles;
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
theme
 
that
 
stretches
 
across
 
redemptive
 
history,
 
woven
 
into
 
the
 
Law,
 
highlighted
 
in
 
the
 
Prophets,
 
embodied
 
in
 
Wisdom
 
literature,
 
and
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
the
 
life
 
and
 
ministry
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Indeed, a proper understanding of this fear is essential to spiritual maturity. 
As
 
the
 
Scriptures
 
declare,
 
“The
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
wisdom”
 
(Proverbs
 
9:10).
 
It
 
is
 
both
 
a
 
foundation
 
and
 
a
 
fountain,
 
laying
 
the
 
 8

groundwork for wise living and springing forth in sanctified action. And 
yet,
 
we
 
must
 
ask:
 
what
 
is
 
this
 
fear?
 
Is
 
it
 
noun
 
or
 
verb,
 
disposition
 
or
 
action,
 
dread
 
or
 
devotion?
 
To answer these questions, I have drawn not only from the biblical text but 
also
 
from
 
the
 
original
 
languages
 
in
 
which
 
the
 
Scriptures
 
were
 
given.
 
The
 
Old
 
Testament
 
(written
 
primarily
 
in
 
Hebrew)
 
uses
 
the
 
root
 
word
 
yareʾ
 
to
 
describe
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God,
 
a
 
word
 
that
 
encapsulates
 
reverence,
 
awe,
 
respect,
 
and
 
yes,
 
even
 
trembling.
 
But
 
it
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
fear
 
that
 
drives
 
us
 
from
 
God;
 
rather,
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
fear
 
that
 
draws
 
us
 
to
 
Him
 
with
 
eyes
 
wide
 
open
 
and
 
knees
 
bent
 
low.
 
In
 
the
 
New
 
Testament
 
(written
 
in
 
Greek),
 
phobos
 
and
 
eulabeia
 
extend
 
this
 
meaning,
 
not
 
merely
 
in
 
the
 
context
 
of
 
servile
 
fear,
 
but
 
as
 
a
 
response
 
to
 
God’s
 
holiness,
 
justice,
 
and
 
glory.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
sanctified
 
response
 
to
 
God,
 
who
 
is
 
both
 
consuming
 
fire
 
and
 
perfect
 
love.
 
Throughout the Scriptures, the fear of God is never reduced to a generic 
religious
 
feeling
 
or
 
a
 
vague
 
emotional
 
impulse.
 
It
 
is
 
covenantal.
 
It
 
is
 
relational.
 
It
 
flows
 
from
 
knowing
 
who
 
God
 
is,
 
His
 
character,
 
His
 
majesty,
 
His
 
justice,
 
and
 
His
 
mercy.
 
As
 
we
 
follow
 
the
 
biblical
 
thread,
 
we
 
see
 
that
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God
 
is
 
cultivated
 
when
 
people
 
encounter
 
Him
 
personally:
 
when
 
Abraham
 
obeys
 
without
 
knowing
 
where
 
he’s
 
going;
 
when
 
Moses
 
removes
 
his
 
sandals
 
at
 
the
 
burning
 
bush;
 
when
 
Israel
 
trembles
 
at
 
Sinai
 
yet
 
draws
 
near
 
through
 
blood;
 
when
 
David
 
sings
 
of
 
the
 
holiness
 
of
 
Yahweh;
 
when
 
 9

Isaiah cries “Woe is me!” ; when Jesus, the embodiment of divine 
reverence,
 
teaches
 
that
 
only
 
God
 
is
 
to
 
be
 
feared;
 
and
 
when
 
the
 
early
 
Church
 
“walked
 
in
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
and
 
in
 
the
 
comfort
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost”
 
(Acts
 
9:31).
 
Theologically, the fear of God is eschatological, it points us forward. It is 
not
 
simply
 
a
 
response
 
to
 
God’s
 
present
 
greatness;
 
it
 
is
 
shaped
 
by
 
the
 
knowledge
 
that
 
we
 
must
 
all
 
stand
 
before
 
Him,
 
and
 
that
 
our
 
lives
 
will
 
be
 
weighed
 
in
 
the
 
light
 
of
 
eternity.
 
It
 
teaches
 
us
 
to
 
live
 
purposefully,
 
humbly,
 
and
 
dependently.
 
Not
 
in
 
the
 
dread
 
of
 
condemnation,
 
for
 
the
 
one
 
who
 
is
 
in
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
has
 
passed
 
from
 
death
 
to
 
life,
 
but
 
in
 
the
 
gravity
 
of
 
stewardship
 
and
 
the
 
glory
 
of
 
sonship.
 
As
 
John
 
the
 
Beloved
 
wrote,
 
“Herein
 
is
 
our
 
love
 
made
 
perfect,
 
that
 
we
 
may
 
have
 
boldness
 
in
 
the
 
day
 
of
 
judgment...
 
There
 
is
 
no
 
fear
 
in
 
love;
 
but
 
perfect
 
love
 
casteth
 
out
 
fear:
 
because
 
fear
 
hath
 
torment”
 
(1
 
John
 
4:17-18).
 
Yet
 
this
 
same
 
John
 
also
 
fell
 
as
 
though
 
dead
 
at
 
the
 
feet
 
of
 
the
 
risen
 
Lord
 
in
 
Revelation.
 
He
 
understood
 
the
 
paradox
 
well,
 
perfect
 
love
 
does
 
not
 
cancel
 
holy
 
reverence;
 
it
 
purifies
 
it.
 
We will investigate how the fear of the Lord is cultivated, lost, restored, 
and
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
the
 
believer’s
 
life.
 
From
 
Adam’s
 
hiding
 
in
 
the
 
garden
 
to
 
the
 
trembling
 
of
 
the
 
saints
 
before
 
the
 
throne
 
in
 
glory,
 
this
 
book
 
will
 
explore
 
how
 
God
 
initiates,
 
deepens,
 
and
 
redeems
 
the
 
fear
 
that
 
is
 
due
 
to
 
His
 
name.
 
We
 
will
 
examine
 
the
 
prophetic
 
tradition
 
where
 
the
 
lack
 
of
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God
 
 10

is both a diagnosis of national backsliding and a personal invitation to 
repentance.
 
We
 
will
 
study
 
how
 
Jesus
 
redefines
 
greatness
 
not
 
in
 
terms
 
of
 
religious
 
status
 
but
 
in
 
intimacy
 
with
 
the
 
Father,
 
childlike
 
humility,
 
and
 
awe-filled
 
obedience.
 
And
 
we
 
will
 
see
 
how
 
the
 
apostles,
 
particularly
 
Paul
 
and
 
Peter,
 
exhorted
 
the
 
Church
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
holy
 
fear,
 
not
 
out
 
of
 
terror,
 
but
 
out
 
of
 
the
 
weight
 
of
 
love,
 
grace,
 
and
 
divine
 
calling.
 
My prayer is that as you read, a deep longing will be awakened in you, not 
merely
 
to
 
know
 
about
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God,
 
but
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
it.
 
That
 
your
 
heart
 
will
 
be
 
stirred
 
to
 
worship,
 
your
 
knees
 
drawn
 
to
 
prayer,
 
and
 
your
 
life
 
conformed
 
to
 
the
 
pattern
 
of
 
those
 
who
 
feared
 
the
 
Lord
 
and
 
walked
 
with
 
Him.
 
For
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
theoretical
 
posture;
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
atmosphere
 
in
 
which
 
a
 
life
 
of
 
holiness,
 
humility,
 
and
 
heartfelt
 
obedience
 
is
 
cultivated.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
the
 
enemy
 
of
 
intimacy
 
with
 
God,
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
gateway
 
to
 
it.
 
We live in a time when much of modern Christianity has lost the 
vocabulary
 
of
 
reverence.
 
God
 
is
 
spoken
 
of
 
as
 
friend,
 
helper,
 
life
 
coach,
 
and
 
even
 
business
 
partner,
 
but
 
rarely
 
as
 
holy
.
 
Rarely
 
as
 
King
.
 
Rarely
 
as
 
Judge
.
 
And
 
rarer
 
still
 
as
 
the
 
One
 
before
 
whom
 
angels
 
veil
 
their
 
faces
 
and
 
elders
 
cast
 
their
 
crowns.
 
If
 
ever
 
there
 
was
 
a
 
time
 
when
 
the
 
Church
 
needs
 
to
 
rediscover
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God,
 
it
 
is
 
now.
 
So, let us come with open hearts and attentive spirits. Let us think deeply, 
pray
 
earnestly,
 
and
 
read
 
reverently.
 
This
 
book
 
does
 
not
 
claim
 
to
 
exhaust
 
the
 11

subject, but it does aim to serve as a theological map and a devotional 
guide.
 
I
 
have
 
consulted
 
the
 
Hebrew
 
and
 
Greek
 
texts
 
where
 
necessary,
 
employed
 
biblical
 
typology
 
and
 
redemptive
 
themes,
 
and
 
submitted
 
all
 
to
 
the
 
Word
 
of
 
Truth
 
rightly
 
divided.
 
I ask and receive for you the spirit of wisdom and revelation in the 
knowledge
 
of
 
Him
 
as
 
you
 
read
 
and
 
consider
 
this
 
writing.
 
May
 
your
 
life
 
be
 
marked
 
by
 
the
 
fear
 
that
 
frees,
 
the
 
fear
 
that
 
teaches,
 
the
 
fear
 
that
 
purifies,
 
and
 
the
 
fear
 
that
 
draws
 
us
 
nearer
 
to
 
the
 
One
 
who
 
is
 
altogether
 
holy
 
and
 
altogether
 
loving.
 
 12

The journey that led to the creation of this book began with a conversation, 
a
 
moment
 
that
 
profoundly
 
altered
 
the
 
course
 
of
 
my
 
thinking
 
and
 
deepened
 
my
 
understanding
 
of
 
one
 
of
 
the
 
most
 
foundational
 
aspects
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith:
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord.
 
It
 
was
 
in
 
a
 
casual
 
yet
 
deeply
 
impactful
 
discussion
 
with
 
my
 
dear
 
friend,
 
Apostle
 
Bobby
 
Udosen,
 
that
 
the
 
subject
 
was
 
brought
 
up,
 
almost
 
as
 
if
 
it
 
had
 
never
 
truly
 
crossed
 
my
 
mind
 
before.
 
As
 
he
 
spoke
 
about
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
God,
 
something
 
in
 
that
 
moment
 
resonated
 
within
 
me,
 
striking
 
my
 
heart
 
with
 
the
 
force
 
of
 
a
 
fresh
 
revelation.
 
His words seemed to leap out of the conversation, grabbing my attention in 
a
 
way
 
that
 
was
 
impossible
 
to
 
ignore.
 
It
 
felt
 
as
 
though
 
I
 
had
 
never
 
truly
 
understood
 
the
 
depth,
 
the
 
beauty,
 
and
 
the
 
necessity
 
of
 
this
 
godly
 
fear.
 
I
 
found
 
myself
 
captivated
 
by
 
the
 
idea,
 
and
 
from
 
that
 
point
 
onward,
 
I
 
was
 
compelled
 
to
 
embark
 
on
 
a
 
personal
 
study
 
of
 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord,
 
and
 
how
 
this
 
reverence
 
should
 
be
 
reflected
 
in
 
the
 
life
 
of
 
every
 
believer.
 
The more I studied, the more I realized how profoundly this theme is 
woven
 
throughout
 
Scripture,
 
from
 
the
 
patriarchs
 
of
 
the
 
faith
 
to
 
the
 
teachings
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
Himself.
 
It
 
became
 
apparent
 
that
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
 13

Lord is not a fleeting or peripheral concept, but a core principle that 
informs
 
every
 
facet
 
of
 
our
 
relationship
 
with
 
God
 
and
 
with
 
one
 
another.
 
I
 
felt
 
an
 
urgency
 
to
 
not
 
only
 
study
 
this
 
theme
 
for
 
my
 
own
 
personal
 
growth
 
but
 
also
 
to
 
share
 
it
 
with
 
the
 
congregation
 
of
 
Great
 
Haven
 
Assembly,
 
where
 
I
 
serve
 
as
 
pastor.
 
As
 
I
 
began
 
teaching
 
on
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord,
 
I
 
saw
 
transformation
 
in
 
the
 
hearts
 
of
 
those
 
who
 
listened.
 
The
 
church
 
was
 
being
 
drawn
 
into
 
a
 
deeper,
 
more
 
genuine
 
relationship
 
with
 
God,
 
where
 
reverence
 
and
 
awe
 
were
 
no
 
longer
 
abstract
 
concepts
 
but
 
lived
 
realities.
 
And thus, this book was born. 
The pages of this book are not merely an academic exploration of a 
theological
 
concept.
 
They
 
are
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
experience
 
the
 
life-changing
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
transforms
 
our
 
hearts,
 
our
 
homes,
 
and
 
our
 
churches.
 
As
 
you
 
read,
 
you
 
will
 
be
 
invited
 
into
 
a
 
journey
 
of
 
reflection,
 
one
 
that
 
will
 
challenge
 
your
 
perspectives,
 
deepen
 
your
 
reverence,
 
and
 
inspire
 
a
 
fresh
 
pursuit
 
of
 
holiness
 
and
 
obedience.
 
This
 
book
 
is
 
not
 
simply
 
for
 
those
 
who
 
seek
 
knowledge,
 
but
 
for
 
those
 
who
 
long
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
God’s
 
presence
 
and
 
to
 
live
 
lives
 
that
 
honor
 
Him
 
in
 
all
 
things.
 
Throughout the chapters, you will encounter the lives of biblical figures, 
Abraham,
 
Moses,
 
Joseph,
 
and
 
others,
 
whose
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
shaped
 
the
 
course
 
of
 
history.
 
You
 
will
 
also
 
be
 
confronted
 
with
 
practical
 14

applications of how the fear of God should influence our speech, choices, 
priorities,
 
and
 
relationships.
 
You
 
will
 
see
 
how
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
not
 
something
 
to
 
be
 
dreaded,
 
but
 
a
 
holy
 
reverence
 
that
 
fuels
 
wisdom,
 
purity,
 
and
 
transformation
 
in
 
every
 
believer’s
 
life.
 
As you journey through this book, I pray that you will be awakened to the 
depth
 
of
 
God’s
 
holiness,
 
the
 
beauty
 
of
 
His
 
presence,
 
and
 
the
 
joy
 
of
 
living
 
in
 
awe
 
and
 
reverence
 
before
 
Him.
 
My
 
hope
 
is
 
that
 
this
 
book
 
will
 
be
 
more
 
than
 
a
 
source
 
of
 
knowledge
 
for
 
you.
 
I
 
pray
 
that
 
it
 
will
 
stir
 
a
 
new
 
desire
 
in
 
your
 
heart
 
to
 
live
 
in
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord,
 
to
 
honor
 
God
 
with
 
your
 
life,
 
and
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
His
 
grace.
 
May you encounter the transforming power of the fear of the Lord in ways 
that
 
lead
 
you
 
to
 
a
 
deeper,
 
more
 
intimate
 
relationship
 
with
 
God.
 
May
 
it
 
inspire
 
you
 
to
 
live
 
with
 
wisdom,
 
humility,
 
and
 
integrity,
 
and
 
may
 
it
 
equip
 
you
 
to
 
lead
 
others
 
into
 
a
 
life
 
of
 
reverence,
 
obedience,
 
and
 
joy
 
before
 
our
 
Heavenly
 
Father.
 
Thank you for joining me on this journey. I trust that as you read, the fear 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
will
 
become
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
doctrine
 
to
 
understand,
 
but
 
a
 
reality
 
to
 
experience,
 
a
 
reality
 
that
 
will
 
guide
 
you,
 
shape
 
you,
 
and
 
bring
 
you
 
into
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
God’s
 
calling
 
for
 
your
 
life.
 
Dr. Daniel Folarin 
 15

. 
 
SECTION ONE: 
FOUNDATIONS OF THE FEAR OF THE LORD 
 
 
 
 16

What Is the Fear of God? 
 
The concept of the "fear of God" is one that appears repeatedly throughout 
Scripture,
 
yet
 
it
 
often
 
carries
 
with
 
it
 
a
 
great
 
deal
 
of
 
misunderstanding.
 
What
 
does
 
it
 
mean
 
to
 
fear
 
God?
 
Why
 
is
 
it
 
so
 
central
 
to
 
wisdom,
 
yet
 
seemingly
 
contradictory
 
to
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
God
 
as
 
a
 
loving
 
Father?
 
These
 
questions
 
must
 
be
 
addressed
 
if
 
we
 
are
 
to
 
grasp
 
the
 
true
 
essence
 
of
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord,
 
its
 
power,
 
and
 
its
 
significance
 
for
 
the
 
believer.
 
The Essence of the Fear of God 
The fear of God, often considered an abstract idea or concept, has been 
widely
 
misunderstood
 
in
 
the
 
Christian
 
community.
 
On
 
one
 
hand,
 
the
 
Bible
 
seems
 
to
 
say
 
"do
 
not
 
fear"
 
(Isaiah
 
41:10),
 
yet
 
on
 
the
 
other
 
hand,
 
it
 
clearly
 
states
 
that
 
"the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
LORD
 
is
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
wisdom"
 
(Proverbs
 
9:10).
 
The
 
key
 
to
 
understanding
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
lies
 
in
 
discerning
 
the
 
different
 
types
 
of
 
fear
 
presented
 
in
 
Scripture.
 
As humans, we tend to view fear as an inherently negative or destructive 
emotion,
 
something
 
to
 
avoid.
 
However,
 
the
 
Bible
 
shows
 
that
 
the
 
fear
 
of
 
the
 17

Lord is not just an emotion but a foundational principle for understanding 
God
 
and
 
living
 
wisely.
 
If
 
the
 
Bible
 
simultaneously
 
encourages
 
us
 
to
 
fear
 
and
 
to
 
not
 
fear,
 
we
 
must
 
learn
 
the
 
context
 
in
 
which
 
each
 
type
 
of
 
fear
 
applies.
 
What,
 
then,
 
is
 
the
 
right
 
kind
 
of
 
fear
 
that
 
leads
 
to
 
wisdom
 
and
 
life?
 
Exploring the Hebrew Word for "Fear" 
In the Old Testament, the primary Hebrew word used for "fear" is yirah 
(
יָרֵא
.)
 
This
 
word
 
encompasses
 
a
 
broad
 
spectrum
 
of
 
meanings,
 
ranging
 
from
 
simple
 
fear
 
or
 
terror
 
to
 
reverence,
 
awe,
 
and
 
reverential
 
worship.
 
Yirah
 
is
 
often
 
translated

based
 
on

At

near,

(as

12:28).
 
This new fear is formative. It teaches obedience, cultivates humility, and

intimacy
 
with
 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
the
 
kind
 
of
 
fear
 
Jesus
 
embodied

a
 
cringing

delight
 
in
 
doing
 
the
 
Father’s

(Isa.

This
 
fear

is not expelled by grace but purified by it. Grace does not make us flippant;

a. Worship : The Cross should inspire reverent worship. We approach God

5:11
 
states,

the

men."
 
Our

d. Suffering : The fear of God stabilizes us in trials. When lesser fears

,

fear
 
of
 
God
 
,

e. Assurance : The same grace that teaches us to fear also assures us of

Romans

The reframing of fear through the Cross invites us

A New Covenant and a New Heart 
Under the Old Covenant, laws were external. They were written on tablets 
of

The

were

to
 
fear
 
God,
 
but
 
their

In

"But this shall be the covenant that I will make with the house

Father"

under

as
 
sons
 
who
 
respect
 
the
 
holiness
 
of
 
a
 
loving
 
Father.

Throughout the book of Acts, we see that the early Church was marked by

sense

reverence:
 
"And great fear came upon all the church, and upon as many

these

Introduction 
The apostle John, often referred to as the apostle of love, penned one of the

theologically

The Greek word translated as "fear" in this passage is phobos , a term that

denote

crippling

is

writing about judgment and assurance. The

verse

is

"fear"
 
in

not
 
the

fear

God
 
that
 
leads
 
to
 
obedience
 
and

the
 
dread
 
of

and

The torment ( kolasis ) John refers to is related to the expectation of 
judgment.
 
As
 
scholar

Carson

“The

in

Perfect
 
love,

does
 
not
 
annihilate
 
reverence,
 
it
 
dispels
 
the
 
kind
 
of

that
 
sees
 
God

angry

instead

Father.

This distinction is consistent throughout Scripture. Proverbs tells us that 
"the

of

Lord

(Prov.

How

with

We must understand that Scripture presents multiple facets of fear. The Old

like

and

,

to

Testament

.
 
The

,

Far from contradicting John’s message, Jesus’ example affirms it. The fear

In Acts 9:31, we read: "Then had the churches rest... and walking in the

of

but because of it. Church fathers like Augustine and Chrysostom saw the

“Let

Reformer John Calvin distinguished between

3. The Practical Outworking of Apostolic Fear 
Both Paul and Peter saw the fear of the Lord as:

5:11,
 
fear
 
motivated

and
 
Peter
 
taught

fear

fear,

fear

and
 
Peter

and
 
glorify
 
God
 
in
 
holy
 
fear.

Pictorial Representation of Yirah 
The fear and fearfulness are usually each Yirah which is spelt " Yood 
Reysh
 
Aleph
 
Hey
."
 
Ancient Language 
 
Modern Hebrew 
 
Yood is the picture of the hand or arm and means "to work or a mighty work or deed." 
Reysh is the picture of the head and means "the leader, the master, or the prince." 
 191

Aleph is the picture of the ox and means "the strong leader, the head of the family, or God the Father." 
Hey is the picture of the man with uplifted arms and means "to behold, to pay attention to what follows, or the Holy Spirit as the 
Revelator."

often
 
overlooked

of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith,

for
 
God.

written
 
in
 
2021,

book
 
sought
 
to

the
 
foundation
 
for

of

of
